title: Why we still write postcards
summary: A short editorial about why some people still mail postcards in an age of one-second email — and what a postcard can say that an inbox cannot.
body:
Email is fast. A message arrives in one second. We send dozens of them a day. So why do some people still send postcards?

A postcard takes effort. You walk to a shop. You choose one card from a hundred. You think about who will read it. You buy a stamp. You write only fifty words because there is no more space. You stand in line at the post office. The card travels for a week. Then it arrives in someone's mailbox.

That is exactly the point. Email is everywhere. A postcard is one. Email is free. A postcard costs a stamp. Email forgets where it came from. A postcard has a picture of the harbour, the bridge, the dog in the square. It says: I was there. I thought of you.

A postcard does not say much. The space is small. But it tells the reader: somebody used real time on me. Somebody had a hand and a pen. Somebody waited a week to make me smile.

A grandmother does not need a thesis. She needs to know you saw a yellow boat and you thought of her.

If you have ten minutes today, write one. Stamps still exist.

annotations.meaningVi: luận văn / bài dài và chi tiết

grammarNotes.title: Generic 'you' (meaning 'people in general')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tiếng Anh, 'you' không phải lúc nào cũng chỉ một người nghe cụ thể — đôi khi nó nghĩa là 'bất kỳ ai / người ta nói chung'. Trong bài: 'You walk to a shop. You choose one card from a hundred. You buy a stamp.' Tác giả không nói về người đọc cụ thể nào; ý là 'bất kỳ ai gửi bưu thiếp cũng làm các bước này'. Cách dùng này tạo cảm giác gần gũi, dễ chia sẻ. Tiếng Việt thường dịch là 'mình', 'ta', hoặc 'người ta'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In English, 'you' is not always one specific listener — it often means 'anyone, in general'. The editorial uses generic 'you' throughout: 'You walk to a shop. You choose one card.' The writer is not talking about a particular reader; she means 'anyone who sends a postcard does this'. It feels warmer than 'one walks to a shop.'

translationVi:
Email nhanh. Một tin nhắn đến trong một giây. Chúng ta gửi hàng chục cái mỗi ngày. Vậy sao một số người vẫn gửi bưu thiếp?

Gửi bưu thiếp tốn công. Bạn đi đến một cửa hàng. Bạn chọn một tấm thiếp trong số một trăm. Bạn nghĩ ai sẽ đọc nó. Bạn mua một con tem. Bạn chỉ viết được khoảng năm mươi chữ vì hết chỗ. Bạn xếp hàng ở bưu điện. Tấm thiếp đi suốt một tuần. Rồi nó vào hộp thư của ai đó.

Đó chính là điểm hay. Email ở khắp nơi. Một tấm bưu thiếp là một. Email miễn phí. Bưu thiếp phải có tem. Email không nhớ nó đến từ đâu. Bưu thiếp có hình bến cảng, cây cầu, con chó ngoài quảng trường. Nó nói: tớ đã ở đó. Tớ đã nghĩ đến cậu.

Bưu thiếp không nói nhiều. Chỗ viết ít. Nhưng nó nói với người đọc: có một người đã bỏ ra thời gian thật cho mình. Có một bàn tay và một cái bút. Có một người đã chờ một tuần để khiến mình mỉm cười.

Một người bà không cần một luận văn. Bà chỉ cần biết bạn đã thấy một con thuyền vàng và bạn đã nghĩ đến bà.

Nếu hôm nay bạn có mười phút, hãy viết một tấm. Tem vẫn còn được bán.

questions.prompt: How fast is email, according to the editorial?
options: A message arrives in one second.
A message arrives in one hour.
Email is slower than a postcard.

questions.prompt: About how many words fit on a postcard?
questions.explanation: 'You write only fifty words because there is no more space.'

questions.prompt: Roughly how long does a postcard travel?
Which of these is a contrast the writer draws between email and postcards?
options: Email is everywhere; a postcard is one.
Email is heavy; a postcard is light.
Email is loud; a postcard is silent.
Email is short; a postcard is long.
questions.explanation: 'Email is everywhere. A postcard is one.'
questions.hint: Look at the paired sentences in paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: What two things does the writer say a postcard has that email does not?
options: A picture from a place, and a stamp (a small cost)
A phone number and an address
A secret code and a key
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 talks about the stamp and the picture (the harbour, the bridge, the dog in the square).
questions.hint: Look at what is on a postcard physically.

questions.prompt: What does a postcard tell the reader, even if it does not say much?
options: Somebody used real time on me, had a hand and a pen, and waited a week to make me smile.
questions.hint: Look at the lines about 'real time' and 'a hand and a pen.'

questions.prompt: What does 'A grandmother does not need a thesis' mean?
options: Old people cannot read long things.
A grandmother does not need a long, formal text — a small line about a yellow boat is enough.
questions.explanation: The next sentence: 'She needs to know you saw a yellow boat and you thought of her.'
questions.hint: Look at the contrast with the small concrete detail.

questions.prompt: What does the writer ask the reader to do?
options: If you have ten minutes today, write one postcard.
questions.explanation: 'If you have ten minutes today, write one. Stamps still exist.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the editorial NOT claiming?
options: Email is useless and should be abandoned.
A postcard carries a sense of real time spent by the sender.
A small message can matter if it shows care.
questions.explanation: The writer never says email is useless — only that a postcard does something different.
questions.hint: Look for the option that exaggerates the writer's claim.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the editorial?
options: Postcards survive because the slow, physical effort behind them is part of what they say.
Email should be banned by law.
Yellow boats are the only thing worth writing about.
questions.explanation: The writer argues that 'somebody used real time on me' is the postcard's message.
questions.hint: Think about what the postcard is really telling the reader.

cloze.template:
Email is fast. A message arrives in one {{b1}}. We send dozens of them a day. So why do some people still send postcards?

A postcard takes effort. You walk to a {{b2}}. You choose one card from a hundred. You think about who will read it. You buy a {{b3}}. You write only fifty words because there is no more space. You stand in line at the post office. The card travels for a {{b4}}. Then it arrives in someone's mailbox.

That is exactly the point. Email is everywhere. A postcard is {{b5}}. Email is free. A postcard costs a stamp. Email forgets where it came from. A postcard has a {{b6}} of the harbour, the bridge, the dog in the square. It says: I was there. I thought of you.

A postcard does not say much. The space is small. But it tells the reader: somebody used real {{b7}} on me. Somebody had a hand and a pen. Somebody waited a week to make me {{b8}}.

A grandmother does not need a thesis. She needs to know you saw a yellow {{b9}} and you thought of her.

If you have ten minutes today, write one. Stamps still {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: You walk to a shop for the card.
Email everywhere; a postcard is one.
A postcard has a picture of a place.
Real time spent by the sender.
Waited a week to make me smile.
She wants to know you saw a yellow boat.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The editorial argues that the slow effort behind a postcard is part of its message. Is there a habit in your life — a phone call, a handwritten note, a home-cooked meal — that you keep doing the slow way because the slowness is the point?
